entry.title: New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the July 2026 shipment
entry.content:
We have just shipped new versions of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages. We have implemented a couple of feature enhancements, mainly based on customer demand or stuff we missed out the last time. A major feature that we have shipped is around retry handling, actually the option to skip retry for exceptions where a retry does not make sense.

The following new versions have been shipped:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound version 1.0.8
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines version 1.0.5
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound version 1.0.4
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates version 1.2.0

And here the list of new features at a glance:

  • Skip Retry Handling: The retry behavior has been changed. For some errors where an automatic message retry would not make sense, the retry is skipped and the message is directly stored in the dead letter queue. For example, if a message mapping fails, it doesn't make sense to automatically retry until we reach the maximum number of retries. If we won't fix the mapping, the message will constantly fail. So, in this case, the retry is skipped and the failed message is directly stored in the dead letter queue. For the skip retry handling we do support three options to configure the skip retry behaviour: hard-coded (this is the default), lockup a value mapping that maps exception classes to a boolean true/false, and lookup a profile in the Partner Directory. In this context, we have shipped a value mapping as part of the templates package as well as a helper flow to sync the value mapping entries with the Partner Directory. For more details, check out this blog post.
  • Sender and receiver queue names shown in MPL: For all asynchronous pipeline flows, the sender and receiver queue names have been added to the custom header attributes so that you have full transparency about the JMS queues that the messages pass through.
  • Display original error message when max retry has been exceeded: For generic integration flows that may do a split like receiver determination, interface determination and the integrated messaging runtime, we have to do one more retry if a message fails and the maximum number of retries have been exceeded. Otherwise, we may create duplicates. So far, the original error message was lost after the additional retry. Now, you have the option to configure the OData API details of your tenant for the generic integration flows affected to fetch the original error message from the message processing log.
  • Restore payload, headers, attachments in case of retry: In case that an erroneous message is stored in the dead letter queue, the payload, all headers, and all attachments if any are restored to ensure that they are in the state that they had at the JMS queue inbound. Before, this was not always guaranteed.
  • Flexible landscape stages scenario coverage: In the generic XI inbound processing flow, flexible landscape stages is now also supported for the Point-to-Point bypass option. Somehow, we missed this out.
  • Response body nulled for XI inbound: For asynchronous messages using the generic XI inbound processing flow, the body of the response is nulled after having stored the message in the JMS queue. This was reported by a customer that faced issues in the sender system in case of large messages.
  • Allowed header list enhanced: The allowed header list of the generic XI inbound processing flow and the generic IDoc inbound processing flow has been enhanced based on customer demand.
  • XI/IDoc connection parameters via business system or configuration scenario in Partner Directory: For the generic XI outbound processing flow and the generic IDoc outbound processing flow, the option maintaining the connection parameters in the Partner Directory has been enhanced. The parameters can be now maintained as string parameters of a partner ID representing a business system. Furthermore, some connection parameters can be also maintained as string parameters of a partner ID representing a configuration scenario. So far, this was only supported via string parameters in the corresponding business system alias.
  • XI delivery assurance settings revised: For the generic XI outbound processing flow, the delivery assurance settings to determine the XI Message ID have been revised to ensure that a proper Universally Unique Identifier (UUID) is passed to the receiver system. For this, the value of the UniqueID header is mapped to the header SapMessageIdEx and the XI adapter now uses Reuse instead of Map.
  • XI message ID from end to end: Furthermore, in case of an XI to XI scenario, you have the option to pass the sender XI message ID from end-to-end. See Pass XI message ID from end-to-end.
  • IDoc unique ID revised: For the generic IDoc outbound processing flow, the processing settings to determine a unique ID have been revised. For this, the value of the UniqueID header is mapped to the header SapMessageId and the IDoc adapter now uses Reuse instead of Map.
  • Display payload size: For the generic outbound processing flow, you have the option to measure and display the size of the original payload.

Please update the packages in your Design area to benefit from the changes. We recommend to update your dev and test landscape first and to test there before updating the content on your prod tenant. Though we run extensive regression tests and new feature tests before publishing a new version. We even have extended our regression tests by implementing further tests and checks such as checking the number of completed, failed, and escalated logs, including negative tests, restarting a failed message from the dead letter queue (by leveraging the new OData API for the JMS queues), comparing custom status, comparing custom header properties, comparing error messages, etc.

entry.title: Pass XI message ID from end-to-end
entry.content:
With the new version of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages, see New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the July 2026 shipment, we have enhanced the generic XI inbound processing flow as well as the generic XI outbound processing flow to be able to pass the XI message ID from end-to-end. This enhancement was actually added based on a customer requirement. For specific scenarios where both the sender and the receiver use the XI 3.0 protocol, the customer likes to keep the sender XI message ID the same like in the receiving system to be able to track the message from end-to-end.

You can enable the feature for a specific configuration scenario by maintaining the string parameter PassMessageIdFromE2E in the partner ID representing your configuration scenario in the Partner Directory.

We intentionally did not introduce this feature as a global setting because you need to use this setting with caution. Wrongly used, this may lead to data loss. For example, if you duplicate or split up your message in your scenario-specific outbound processing flow that calls the generic XI outbound flow and each split message is sent to the same receiver, only the first message will be processed in the receiver system, all other messages are seen as duplicates in the receiver system because they have the same XI message ID which was successfully processed before and hence they will be discarded leading to data loss. We have implemented a check which will only work when using an iterative splitter or a generic splitter in your scenario-specific outbound processing flow to split up the message. In this case, even if the parameter PassMessageIdFromE2E is set, this setting will be ignored and a new unique ID for each split message is generated. If you use whatever other way to split up the message, the check won't work. So, as said, use this with caution and enable it only for use cases where you are sure that this won't lead to any harm.

So, now let's check how this can be configured and how this behaves. I have setup a configuration scenario of pattern Content-Based Router. For one receiver, I run a one-to-many message mapping to split the message. For another receiver, I only pass one message to the receiver.

In case you are not familiar with scenarios using the generic XI outbound processing flow, check out this blog post Generic XI Outbound flow in Cloud Integration Pipeline. For a better understanding, I schematically show you the message flow:

Sender --via XI protocol--> Generic XI Inbound Processing flow --via JMS adapter--> First JMS Queue --via JMS adapter--> Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow --via JMS adapter--> Second JMS Queue --via JMS adapter--> Generic Outbound Processing  flow --via ProcessDirect--> Scenario-specific Outbound Processing flow --via ProcessDirect--> Generic XI Outbound Processing flow --via XI protocol--> Receiver

So, in the Generic XI Inbound Processing flow I fetch the XI message ID from the Sender, store it in a header and route this through all flows. The Generic XI Inbound Processing flow stores the message in the first JMS queue where it is picked up by the Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow. The Integrated Messaging Pipeline flow carries out the xpath conditions to determine the receiver/s and then stores the message in the second JMS queue for each receiver. In our case, we always have one receiver only at the same time. The Generic Outbound Processing flow picks up the message/s from the second JMS queue and now tries to deliver this to the receiver. It calls the Scenario-specific Outbound Processing flow via ProcessDirect where the actual mapping is carried out and eventually the XI connection parameters are set. The connection to the Receiver is then done via the Generic XI Outbound Processing flow which acts as a sort of channel. If configured, we use the XI message ID from the Sender to create the XI message ID at the Receiver.

So, the screenshot below shows the scenario-specific integration flow where we split up the message. As you can see, we run a one-to-many message mapping, the result is a multi message which is embedded in Messages nodes. We need to get rid of the additional nodes and split up the message into multiple messages. This we do using an iterative splitter. The iterative splitter by the way adds a specific header to the exchange which we pass to the next flow which is the generic XI outbound processing flow where we check if this header is present and if so discard the use of the sender XI message ID. Furthermore, in a content modifier we need to add the Camel split index to the unique ID header so that each split message has a unique ID.

And the following screenshot shows the scenario-specific integration flow with a simple 1:1 mapping resulting in one single message. In this case, we can keep the unique ID header unchanged. But it won't be used anyway because we will pass the XI message ID from the sender to the XI receiver adapter in the generic XI outbound processing flow anyway.

I like to enable the feature for my scenario, at least for those cases were I do not do a split. In the Partner Directory, I add a new string parameter with ID PassMessageIdFromE2E and value true to the partner ID representing my configuration scenario.

Now, I run some tests. First, let's see how the end-to-end XI message ID feature works. In this case, in the message monitor I click on the Application Message ID and this will then automatically filter all logs that belong to the same Application Message ID. Here, the Application Message ID is actually the sender XI message ID. So, you can see that both, the generic XI inbound processing flow as well as the generic XI outbound processing flow have the same XI message ID. I only mocked the sender and receiver but in a real scenario the corresponding messages in the sender and the receiver system would be the same.

For another test message that I sent, the split is carried out. Here, I now click on the Correlation ID to be able to see all logs of the same exchange. Since we did a split, you see two logs of the generic XI outbound processing flow. If I clicked on the Application Message ID, you would not see all logs because the generic XI outbound processing flows have a different XI message ID and hence Application Message ID. Here, the check popped in. Because we did a split in the flow before, the unique ID header is used to generate the XI message ID instead of using the sender XI message ID. You remember, the unique ID header was modified by adding the slit index, so this ensures that the XI message IDs of the split messages differ. So, in this case you can't track the XI message ID from end-to-end but at least we add the sender XI message ID to the custom header properties. If we identify a split, we store the sender XI message ID in the custom header ParentIdBeforeMessageSplit.

Hope this new feature is of value for you. As usually, feedback is highly appreciated.

entry.title: New Skip Retry Handling feature in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration with the July 2026 shipment
entry.content:
In some error cases, an automatic retry doesn’t help, it just adds load on the tenant runtime without fixing anything. For those cases, you can skip the standard retry handling and send the failed messages directly to the Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). That’s exactly the purpose of the new "Skip Retry Handling" feature in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration (July 2026 shipment). This blog covers when retries are skipped by default, and how you can adjust the behavior globally or per scenario.

How “Skip Retry Handling” Helps

  • The tenant stops wasting runtime on errors that will never succeed.
  • Failures land in the DLQ quickly, so you can actually see what broke instead of digging through retry noise.
  • You can leave the defaults alone, or tune the behavior globally or per scenario.

Where Retries Are Skipped by Default

For the integrated messaging runtime (including the Generic Receiver Determination and Interface Determination flows), retries are skipped and messages go straight to the DLQ in these cases:
  • Partner ID could not be determined.
  • Configuration option not supported.
  • Receiver could not be determined when “Receiver Not Found” is set to Error.
  • Receiver interface could not be determined.
For all other error cases (in these components), the standard retry handling applies.

Tuning Skip Retries in Generic Outbound Processing

For the Generic Outbound Processing flow, you can fine-tune how skip retry handling works. Two control levels exist:
  • Global settings (apply to all copied Generic Outbound flows—unless overridden)
  • Scenario-specific settings (per integration scenario via Partner Directory)
By default, the global skip is enabled:
  • Parameter: SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true

Configuration options

OptionHow to ActivateWhat it doesWhen to use?
Default (Hard-coded)SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = DefaultSkips retries only for Mapping errors (com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException)Good baseline if mapping errors are your main non-transient case
Global via Value MappingSkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping and deploy the “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration”Centralizes per-exception-class settings in a value mappingWhen you want one global control point for all scenarios
Global via Partner ID ProfileSkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory plus a Partner ID profileLets you define a skip-retry profile (exception classes set to true) and reuse across copies of the outbound flowIdeal for multi-unit tenants where each unit needs its own profile
Scenario-specific ProfileSet SkipRetriesProfile on the Partner ID that represents the scenarioOverrides global settings just for that scenarioWhen certain scenarios need stricter or looser retry rules

Examples

Example 1: Mapping Errors

Symptoms:

  • Messages fail with mapping exceptions.
  • Reprocessing without changing the mapping yields the same error. Messages can't be edited directly as they could be done in SAP Process Orchestration.
Setup:
  • Keep SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
  • Keep SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Result:
  • Mapping exceptions (com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException) go straight to DLQ.
  • The Integration/Operations teams can focus on either fixing the mapping or fixing the functional data.

Example 2: Receiver Not Found

Scenario:

  • You enforce strict routing rules—if a receiver cannot be determined and “Receiver Not Found” is set to Error, there is no fallback.
Result:
  • Integrated messaging runtime already skips retries and puts the failure in DLQ. No extra config required.

Example 3: Scenario-based Retry Profiles

Attach profiles to scenarios so each scenario can apply its own skip behavior without duplicating flows.

Scenario:

  • Scenario A (“Order-to-Cash Outbound”): skip retries for mapping errors only.
  • Scenario B (“Billing to CRM”): skip retries for mapping errors and known and expected script failures
Setup (Partner Directory profiles):
  • Create profile “SkipProfile_Scenario_O2C”: set com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException = true.
  • Create profile “SkipProfile_Scenario_BillingCRM”: set com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException = true and javax.script.ScriptException = true.
  • On the Partner ID for “Order-to-Cash Outbound”:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_O2C
  • On the Partner ID for “Billing to CRM Sync”:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_BillingCRM
Result:
  • Scenario A (“Order-to-Cash Outbound”) skips only mapping exceptions and retries everything else as usual.
  • Scenario B (“Billing to CRM”) skips mapping and javax.script.ScriptException, while still retrying potentially transient connection issues (for example, timeouts).

Example 4: A Single Scenario That Needs Stricter Skips

Sometimes one scenario needs stricter rules than the rest. A classic case: SOAP faults on partner onboarding, where the fault usually means the payload or contract is wrong and no amount of retrying is going to change that.
Scenario:
  • “Partner Onboarding (SOAP)” must never retry on SOAP faults because these typically point to permanent contract or payload issues.
Setup:
  • Create a skip-retry profile "SkipProfile_Scenario_OnboardingStrict" with org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault = true.
  • In the Partner ID representing this scenario, add:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = SkipProfile_Scenario_OnboardingStrict
Result:
  • Only this scenario overrides the global policy and skips org.apache.cxf.binding.soap.SoapFault.

Configuration Options and Behavior

1) Default (Hard-coded)

  • In your Generic Outbound Processing flow:
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Behavior: Only mapping exceptions skip retry (class com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException).

2) Global Value Mapping

  • Set SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping.
  • Prerequisite: Deploy “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration” from the “Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates” package.
  • Default behavior of the value mapping:
    • com.sap.xi.mapping.camel.XiMappingException → true
    • All other exceptions → false
  • To add/adjust behavior, edit values in the value mapping and redeploy.
When to choose: You want one centralized table to govern skip behavior across the tenant. Note you can't deploy a copy of the provided value mapping with the same combination of agency and scheme, choose this option if you like to customize the skip retry handling globally across all scenarios.

 

3) Global Partner ID Profile (via Partner Directory)

  • Create a Partner ID that acts as your global skip-retry profile.
  • Add one string parameter per exception class to skip:
    • Parameter ID = fully qualified exception class name
    • Value = true
  • In your Generic Outbound flow:
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory
    • SkipRetriesGlobal_SourcePidProfile = <YourProfileName>
You can quickly bootstrap this profile from the value mapping using the helper iFlow (below).

 

Helper iFlow: Auto-generate a Profile From the Value Mapping

  • Use “Pipeline v2 - Partner Directory Upload Helper - Skip Retry Configuration.”
  • Prerequisites:
    • In SAP BTP Cockpit Create a Service Instance for “SAP Process Integration Runtime” with plan apias described on SAP Help Portal and a corresponding service key. The required role for the service instance is AuthGroup_TenantPartnerDirectoryConfigurator.
  • Configure and deploy with:
    • API_URL = tenant-specific OData API URL (from your service key)
    • API_Credential_Alias = alias of the OAuth2 Client Credentials
    • PartnerDirectory_PID = target profile name (default SAP_Integration_Suite_SkipRetryGlobalProfile)
    • ValueMapping_TechnicalID = com.sap.integration.cloud.pipeline.v2.value.mapping.skipretry (default)
    • ValueMapping_Version = 1.0.1 (default)
The helper runs once per deployment, creates the Partner Directory profile, reads the value mapping, and writes exception class parameters with true/false. You can fine-tune afterward.

 

4) Scenario-specific Profile (Overrides Global)

  • First, create a skip-retry profile in Partner Directory (as above).
  • In the Partner ID representing your scenario, add:
    • SkipRetriesProfile = <YourScenarioProfile>
This setting overrides any global policy for that scenario.

 

Points to consider

  • Don't over-skip:
    • Timeouts, throttling, HTTP 5xx from backends usually resolve themselves on the next attempt. If you put these exception classes in the skip list, you’ll create incidents that would have healed on their own.
  • Copying value mappings incorrectly:
    • You can’t deploy a copy with the same agency/scheme. If you need variety across teams, prefer Partner Directory profiles.
  • Forgetting scenario overrides:
    • If a SkipRetriesProfile is set on a scenario’s Partner ID, it overrides global behavior—document and audit these.
  • Nobody's watching the DLQ
    • Skipping retries only helps if someone watches and acts on the DLQ. Ensure processes for analysis, ticketing, and replay are in place. If you’d rather park these messages somewhere other than the DLQ, take a look at the Process Integration Pipeline Extension - Restart via Data Store Community Package that allows you to store the messages in Data Stores. There’s a separate blog post covering it.

Configuration Summary

Global default (hard-coded):
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = Default
Global via Value Mapping:
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = ValueMapping
Global via Partner Directory:
SkipRetriesGlobal_Enabled = true
SkipRetriesGlobal_Source = PartnerDirectory
SkipRetriesGlobal_SourcePidProfile = SAP_Integration_Suite_SkipRetryGlobalProfile // or your profile
Scenario-specific override (on the scenario’s Partner ID):
SkipRetriesProfile = <YourScenarioProfile>

FAQs

  • What happens to skipped messages?
    • They go straight to the Dead Letter Queue (DLQ), where you can analyze and replay after fixing the cause. These messages will have the Custom Status "RetrySkipped".
  • Can I combine global and scenario-specific settings?
    • Yes. The scenario-specific profile overrides the global setting for that scenario only.
  • What’s the fastest way to create a global profile?
    • Use the helper iFlow “Pipeline v2 - Partner Directory Upload Helper - Skip Retry Configuration” to auto-generate a Partner Directory profile from the value mapping.
  • Do I need to copy the value mapping to change it?
    • No. Use the provided “Pipeline V2 - Value Mapping - Skip Retry Configuration” and adjust mapped values. If you need different configuration by unit or flow, use Partner Directory profiles.

Wrap-up

The "Skip Retry Handling" feature helps you avoid retrying messages that will never succeed. There are three ways to set it up:

  • Stick with the defaults: Mapping errors get skipped automatically. No additional configuration required.
  • Use Value Mapping: One place to control skip behavior across the whole tenant.
  • Use Partner Directory profiles: Best when different scenarios or teams need different rules.
Start with whatever’s closest to your setup and adjust as you learn which errors in your landscape actually need to be skipped.

entry.title: Tolerance defined 0 in the delivery
entry.content: the tolerance in the delivery doc. = 0. although the quantity of the delivery item is bigger than in the same item in the order. How can it be ?

entry.title: Material BOM Migration (2602) - BOM Item Document Assignment - Error 29 356 although complete docume
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am currently migrating Material BOMs in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Release 2602) using the Migration Cockpit. 
I am trying to migrate a BOM item with Item Category = D (Document).

Issue Description

The referenced Document Info Record (DIR) already exists in the system and can be assigned manually to the BOM without any issue. 
However, during the Migration Simulation, the process fails with the following error:

  • Message Class: 29
  • Message Number: 356
  • "Please enter complete document key"

Document Information

The document exists in the system with the following key:

  • Document Type: ZBO
  • Document Number: 10000000003
  • Document Version: A
  • Document Part: 000
  • Status: H3 (Released)

Migration Template Details

BOM Item

  • BOM Usage = 1
  • Alternative BOM = 2
  • Item ID = 00000002
  • Item Category = D

BOM Item Document Assignment

The following fields are maintained:

  • Material Number
  • Plant
  • BOM Usage
  • Alternative BOM
  • Item ID = 00000002
  • Document Type = ZBO
  • Document Number = 10000000003
  • Document Version = A
  • Document Part = 000

Verification Already Performed

  • The Document Info Record exists in the system.
  • The document status is valid (H3 – Released).
  • Manual assignment of the same document to the BOM works successfully.
  • All document key fields are maintained in the BOM Item Document Assignment worksheet.
  • The Item ID in the BOM Item sheet matches the Item ID in the BOM Item Document Assignment sheet.

Question

Has anyone successfully migrated BOM document assignments (Item Category D) using the Material BOM migration object in Release 2602?

Are there any:

  • known restrictions,
  • additional mandatory fields,
  • formatting requirements,
  • or linkage dependencies

between the BOM Item and BOM Item Document Assignment worksheets that could trigger this error?

Since the document exists and can be assigned manually, I am wondering whether there is a specific requirement or limitation related to the migration object itself.

Any feedback or experience on this topic would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best Regards.

entry.title: Re: Transform Your Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation with AI-Assisted Procedures
entry.content: Great content, Jair! A highly timely and essential topic to support us through our daily business challenges. Thanks for sharing your insights with such clarity!

entry.title: Extracting COSP data in S/4HANA for Datasphere/SAC
entry.content:
Hello experts,

In our current project we completed a migration to SAP S/4HANA and we are now starting to use SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud for reporting (we're relatively new to SAP's reporting reporting tools).

Before the migration, our reporting relied on extractions from tables COSP and COSS. From what I understand, in S/4HANA the actuals are now stored in ACDOCA, and based on the available Datasphere business content I found a CDS view that allows me to extract actual data from ACDOCA.

However, I am unsure how to handle the planning data that previously came from COSP and COSS.
Are these planning records now stored in ACDOCP in S/4HANA?
Or do I need to design a custom CDS view or another extraction process to retrieve planning data for COSP reporting?

Any guidance or best practices for handling COSP/COSS planning data in S/4HANA (especially when integrating with Datasphere and SAC) would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance.

entry.title: The Fourth Way to Go Offline in UI5 (And Why the Service Worker Stays Dumb)
entry.content:
Written up after attending "Offline capabilities in UI5 – Let's discuss" at UI5Con, run by Viktor Sperling and Lars Kissel from SAP's UI5 team as an open discussion rather than a one-way talk. The app described below is a real, shipped project — a handheld scanning app for warehouse/inventory operations — with client-identifying details deliberately left out.

Three ideas, three shapes of "offline"

The session's core insight, stated up front, was that "offline" isn't one problem — it has a shape, and the right architecture depends on that shape. They presented three ideas, each solving a distinct scenario:

  • Idea 1 — OData V4 Model as Change Buffer. v4.ODataModel holds changes locally (updateGroupId), disables actions that can't succeed offline, submits once back online (submitBatch). Connectivity is actively probed — a fetch/AbortController ping, not just navigator.onLine. Scenario: a commuter on a train, signal drops between two stations. Duration: seconds to minutes.
  • Idea 2 — Service Worker as OData Proxy. The service worker becomes an OData-aware proxy: it parses $batch and read requests, resolves query options, keeps its own IndexedDB store and changelog, and rebuilds a combined $batch on reconnect. Scenario: a field worker visiting customers, connection comes and goes. Duration: minutes to hours.
  • Idea 3 — JSONModel with Explicit Sync. An explicit download → work → submit workflow. JSONModel as the UI model, a v4.ODataModel downloads the required entity sets up front, changes go into a changelog, submitted via Context#setProperty/#delete. Scenario: a traveler on a flight, working for hours in airplane mode. Duration: hours to days.

Idea 1 is elegant if you can stay inside the OData V4 model's own change-tracking. Idea 3 matches the shape of what we needed almost exactly — an explicit, user-initiated offline session, not an accidental one.

Idea 2 is the one worth pausing on, because it's easy to misread it as "just cache the OData responses." It doesn't. It builds its own IndexedDB store and changelog inside the service worker, and re-implements a slice of the OData protocol there — parsing $batch payloads, matching query options against locally held entity sets, replaying a changelog into a fresh $batch on reconnect. That's a genuinely more sophisticated design than an HTTP cache, and it's also exactly why it didn't fit our case: it means OData-protocol knowledge and business logic now live inside a service worker — a context that's already awkward to debug, awkward to unit test, and now also carries backend-shaped logic.

The session closed with a "Pain Points" slide worth quoting directly, because it names this tension independent of any particular implementation:

Back-end logic in the client — understanding the OData protocol, $filter options that quickly become complex, resolving navigation properties locally, duplicating business logic, OData Actions, server-side calculated fields.

Conflicts at sync time — concurrent modifications or business-rule violations, and expired authentication or session tokens, refresh before sync.

That first point is the crux of this whole post: the problem isn't "service workers shouldn't hold data." The problem is putting protocol-aware business logic inside a service worker specifically. The approach below solves the same problem idea 2 solves — resilience across a flaky connection — without paying that price.

Our shape of offline

Before the architecture, the shape of the problem, because it explains every choice below:

  • Runs on ruggedized handheld scanners with genuinely intermittent connectivity — sometimes hours without a signal, not a brief tunnel dip.
  • Scans in bulk via RFID — hundreds of local mutations in a single session before anything needs to reach a server.
  • Submits as a batch at the end of a session, not request-per-scan — the backend should never see a half-finished state.

That's squarely "spontaneous, business-logic-heavy, shared" territory — borrowing the session's own three-axis framing (Preparation: predictable ↔ spontaneous; Interaction complexity: simple form ↔ business logic; Conflict sensitivity: exclusive ↔ shared) — which rules out idea 1 immediately and makes idea 2's in-worker protocol logic feel like the wrong place to carry that weight.

A fourth option: two layers, two jobs

The architecture splits cleanly into two layers that don't know about each other's internals.

Two isolated layers on the device. The service worker never touches OData or $batch; the JSON model/IndexedDB layer owns all sync logic and only reaches the backend at two named moments — a fetch at the start of a session, a $batch submit at the end.

Layer 1 — the shell, and only the shell

The service worker knows nothing about OData, nothing about business entities. Its fetch strategy has three branches:

  • Skip — OData entity sets, lookup endpoints, $batch: always straight to the network, never cached. Stale business data is worse than a failed fetch, and offline resilience for that data lives entirely in the layer below.
  • Network-first with a timeout race — our own app resources (JS/CSS/XML/i18n): online gets fresh content plus a cache update, offline or a timeout falls back to cache. We deliberately avoid navigator.onLine — it lies in both directions. Racing a real fetch() against a timeout is symmetrically correct for "no network" and "server unreachable" alike.
  • Cache-first — third-party/library resources that rarely change.
const NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS = 2000;

function rejectAfter(ms) {
  return new Promise((_, reject) =>
    setTimeout(() => reject(new Error("SW network-timeout")), ms)
  );
}

async function networkFirst(request) {
  const cache = await caches.open(CACHE_NAME);
  try {
    const response = await Promise.race([fetch(request), rejectAfter(NETWORK_TIMEOUT_MS)]);
    if (isCacheable(response)) cache.put(request, response.clone());
    return response;
  } catch {
    const cached = await cache.match(request, { ignoreSearch: true });
    if (cached) return cached;
    throw new Error("no network, no cache");
  }
}

One gotcha worth flagging for anyone building this themselves: cross-origin <script src> loads — like the UI5 runtime, loaded no-cors — come back as opaque responses. response.ok is false even on success, but the body is perfectly cacheable. Skip that check and your library bundles silently never get cached.

Loading UI5 itself deserves its own paragraph. Our bootstrap script picks its UI5 source depending on where it's running: an on-premise deployment loads sap-ui-core.js from the same server (no internet dependency at all), local dev loads from the public SAPUI5 CDN. "Offline-first" starts at the very first <script> tag — if you fetch the UI5 runtime itself from a CDN, your app is, by definition, not offline-capable anywhere without internet access. Those two sources produce different response types (same-origin vs. cross-origin no-cors), which is exactly why the opaque check above matters — without it, cache-first would silently never cache the UI5 runtime in the on-premise case.

Also worth knowing: the service worker doesn't register at all when the app runs inside a Fiori Launchpad — more on why below.

Layer 2 — data, entirely in ordinary application code

IndexedDB is the source of truth; the JSON model is loaded from it on boot and reloaded after every mutation. OData traffic isn't scattered through the app — it happens at exactly two named operations:

  1. Fetch — pull existing records from the backend when online, including a bulk status update so colleagues can see something's been picked up.
  2. Submit — at the end of a session, one $batch per document with everything — header update, item/serial changes — inside a single changeSet, so the backend never sees a half-written state.
private _queueHeaderUpdate(record: Order): void {
  this.model.update(getHeaderPath(record.id), {
    status: "3",
    processedBy: record.processedBy,
    processedAt: toBackendTimestamp()
  }, { merge: true, groupId: BATCH_GROUP, changeSetId: CHANGE_SET });
}

private _submitBatch(): Promise<void> {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    this.model.submitChanges({
      groupId: BATCH_GROUP,
      success: (data) => this._hasError(data) ? reject(data) : resolve(),
      error: reject
    });
  });
}

After a successful sync, the server's response is merged back into the local record — fields the server owns get overwritten, purely local UI state stays as-is.

This is, in workflow terms, closest to idea 3 — download, work, submit — but hardened with a persistent IndexedDB layer underneath instead of a purely in-memory JSONModel, so a page refresh mid-session doesn't throw everything away.

The bit I'm not proud to admit: session expiry

An architecture built around hours of offline work immediately raises a question: what happens to the session/XSRF token when you come back online? Honest answer: we don't have a good one yet.

  • There's no custom 401/403 detection or re-login flow anywhere in the app. Every OData call leans entirely on the OData V2 model's default XSRF fetch/retry behavior.
  • The batch-submit code only checks HTTP status codes (>= 400) to decide whether a sync succeeded — it doesn't distinguish "the server rejected this data" from "the session expired, retry after a token refresh."
  • Practical consequence: if a device has been offline for a day and the underlying session has since expired, the first sync batch after reconnecting fails as a generic error, not a recognizable "please log in again."

Reassurance, not excuse. This isn't a shortcoming unique to this approach. The session's own Pain Points slide names it directly: "expired authentication or session tokens — refresh before sync." Even SAP's own UI5 team presents this as an open problem across all three of their ideas, not something any of them has solved. That took some pressure off having to invent something clever here — naming it honestly is enough.

This isn't a showstopper in practice (logging back in on the device always fixes it), but it is a real gap: this architecture solves the transport problem, not the identity problem. "Offline-first" here does not mean "session-independent." You'd need to layer explicit 401 detection with a clear re-auth prompt on top if that matters for your case.

How the cache invalidates itself

Every build — production or local dev start — stamps a fresh build timestamp into a small generated file, imported into the service worker via importScripts. The service worker script itself never changes between builds; only that one value does.

self.BUILD_VERSION = "1737012345678"; // stamped per build

const CACHE_NAME = "app-v" + self.BUILD_VERSION;

self.addEventListener("activate", (event) => {
  event.waitUntil(
    caches.keys().then((keys) =>
      Promise.all(
        keys.filter((k) => k.startsWith("app-v") && k !== CACHE_NAME)
            .map((k) => caches.delete(k))
      )
    )
  );
});

No semver, no manual version bump to forget — a timestamp per build means the cache invalidates itself automatically on every build/start. Worth noting separately: the app's manifest version is a static marker, unrelated to this cache version or the OData service version — a deliberate decoupling, since it's tempting to conflate all three kinds of "version."

The Fiori Launchpad has its own appetite for the network

A natural question: could this run inside a Fiori Launchpad instead of standalone? In our case, no — and it's worth explaining why, because the reason generalizes.

A Launchpad is itself an application full of network-dependent shell services that have nothing to do with the app you're hosting inside it: personalization (tile layout, saved filters), intent/navigation resolution against a catalog, notifications, enterprise search, usage tracking, user preferences and theming. None of that is "your app" — you don't control it as an app builder, and none of it is designed to gracefully degrade without a network. That's exactly why registering a custom service worker under a Launchpad route conflicts with the shell: it already has its own lifecycle and caching assumptions, and a second service worker layered on top produces, at best, inconsistent behavior. Practically, our app runs standalone via a direct deep link, not as a Launchpad tile.

Speculative — a wish, not a feature. An officially supported "shell offline mode" would let an individual app register its own service worker scoped strictly to its own sub-path — the shell owning the root scope — and would let shell services degrade gracefully (personalization/notifications/search no-op instead of blocking) rather than all-or-nothing. To be clear, I'm not aware this exists today; it's the question this project left open, and a good one to put back to the community.

What about Fiori Elements and OData V4?

Also raised at the session: Fiori Elements with V4 bindings makes offline hard. I think that's right, and it's worth explaining why rather than just asserting it.

Fiori Elements is metadata- and annotation-driven: value helps, filters, side effects after an edit, and draft handling are, at their core, live interactions with the OData V4 service — they're not designed to be replayed from a local cache. sap.ui.model.odata.v4.ODataModel does keep an in-memory cache of already-loaded data, but that's request-level caching within a session, not a persistent offline store with conflict resolution the way a dedicated IndexedDB layer is. Historically, SAP's "real" offline-OData story has lived more at the native/mobile SDK level than in-browser Fiori Elements — worth double-checking the current state of that, since this area moves.

Practically, the options that hold up today, roughly in order of "how much Fiori Elements you keep":

  1. Limit offline to read-mostly reference data (cache value-help lists locally), keep write flows online.
  2. Freestyle islands inside an otherwise Fiori-Elements-driven app for exactly the screens that need to work offline.
  3. Go fully freestyle, as we did — you lose FE's automation (annotations → UI), but gain full control over what lives locally.

Fiori Elements optimizes for speed and consistency while you're online; the moment offline becomes a hard requirement, you're trading away precisely the automation that makes FE valuable — and freestyle starts looking attractive again.

Lessons that cost us a debugging session

  • Silent cache misses on lazy-loaded fragments. Dialogs that only load at runtime via Fragment.load failed silently if they weren't explicitly named in the pre-cache list — an empty dialog, no error — and the symptom only ever showed up under genuine offline testing, never in normal dev with a network.
  • navigator.onLine lies. It's true whenever a network interface exists, not when a server is reachable, and false isn't reliable either. A race between a real fetch() and a timeout is symmetrically correct in both directions.
  • Two UI5 sources, two response types. The same bootstrap code sometimes loads UI5 same-origin, sometimes cross-origin/no-cors — normal vs. opaque responses — and a cacheability check that only looks at response.ok silently misses the second case.
  • A service worker and a Fiori Launchpad shell don't mix. Registering a custom SW under a Launchpad route caused conflicts with the shell's own layer; registration had to be explicitly excluded for those routes.
  • Deliberately no IndexedDB schema migration. On a breaking model change, we chose a new database name over a migration path — old local data is simply ignored, not converted. For a small, known fleet of devices carrying mostly in-transit data, that's a cheaper, safer call than maintaining migration code.
  • Session expiry isn't solved for free by any of this — see above. Worth saying explicitly so the takeaway isn't "offline-first means never thinking about sessions again."

When this pattern fits — and when it doesn't

This isn't "the best option" — it's the option that fits a specific shape of app. The session's own "Technical Aspects" slide offers a genuinely useful way to reason about that shape: three axes — Preparation (predictable ↔ spontaneous), Interaction complexity (simple form ↔ business logic), and Conflict sensitivity (exclusive ↔ shared). Our app sits firmly at the right-hand end of every axis — unpredictable offline moments, non-trivial document logic, multiple colleagues touching the same stock. That's exactly why idea 1, built for predictable and simple cases, wasn't a fit.

Fits well:

  • Apps where users mutate a lot before anything needs to reach a server (scan sessions, inspections, field forms) — the win is decoupling "mutating" from "submitting."
  • Longer, unpredictable offline periods (minutes to hours), not just a brief tunnel dip.
  • Business logic that doesn't fit neatly into a generic entity cache — merges, batch/changeSet semantics, status derived from other fields, locally created records that only get a server ID at sync time.
  • A limited, known set of devices/users, where "no automatic IndexedDB migration" and "manual session recovery" are acceptable trade-offs.

Fits less well:

  • Apps where nearly every action needs a direct server round-trip anyway (real-time collaborative, prices/stock that must be correct right now).
  • Short, second-scale, unprepared offline blips: idea 1 is probably cheaper to build and maintain — you get most of the resilience for free from the model itself.
  • Many concurrent users editing the same records ("shared" on the conflict-sensitivity axis): this pattern solves "one device, local, sync later," not multi-user conflict resolution. Look at CRDTs, optimistic locking with real conflict UI, or just staying online with a good loading state instead.
  • Teams that would rather lean on a standardized offline framework than maintain a custom SW + IndexedDB layer.

A short aside: why not Cordova, Capacitor, or a native SDK?

A fair question: why a PWA/service-worker approach, and not a hybrid or native app that's offline-capable by default? First, a framing correction: a service worker isn't an experimental bet — it's a mature, broadly supported web standard. This isn't "proven vs. experimental," it's "proven vs. proven."

  • Apache Cordova (formerly PhoneGap) wraps a web app in a native webview shell with plugin access to device APIs. Still an active Apache project, but development velocity has been low for years — much of the hybrid ecosystem has moved toward Capacitor. Worth checking current release cadence and plugin health before repeating that as fact, since it dates quickly.
  • Capacitor (Ionic) is the de facto successor for "web app in a native shell" — a similar model, more actively maintained, a more modern plugin architecture.
  • Native, vendor-specific offline SDKs often ship a ready-made offline layer with built-in conflict resolution — at the cost of vendor lock-in: a proprietary data model/SDK, lower portability, and the loss of "it's just a web app, patch with a new build."

We stayed on web/PWA for three reasons: it's built on proven, stable technology rather than a moving target; a new build is a set of static files picked up by the service worker on the next online load — no app-store review, no MDM rollout cycle per patch; and the sync logic is our own readable code against a standard OData service, not a proprietary SDK locking us to one vendor.

Where this leaves things

None of this is a silver bullet, and it isn't meant to replace the session's three ideas — it's meant to sit alongside them as a fourth shape for a fourth kind of problem. If your offline story is short and unprepared, idea 1 will get you further with less code. If you want the app itself to stay entirely unaware of offline handling, idea 2's service-worker-as-proxy is a genuinely clever answer, provided you're comfortable carrying protocol logic in that layer. If your offline sessions are long, deliberate, and business-logic-heavy — ours were — this is the shape that held up.

Thanks to Viktor and Lars for running the session as a discussion instead of a monologue — it's the reason this idea exists in writable form at all. Curious how others have handled the session-expiry gap in particular — that felt like the one open thread nobody in the room had a clean answer for.

entry.title: News from SAP Activate - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608
entry.content:
We are pleased to announce the updates to SAP Activate, including the availability of AI-Assisted tasks. We’re also excited to share the newly refreshed SAP Activate page on sap.com, which offers a clearer, more intuitive overview of the SAP Activate methodology. 

This release delivers a range of enhancements across SAP Activate assets, strengthening implementation guidance and improving usability. As always, our focus remains on simplifying and optimizing the implementation journey to provide a more seamless experience for our users. 

Below are the key highlights for this release:

  • SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: AI-Assisted Tasks Now Available
  • What’s new in Activate Content Library (ACL)
  • SAP Activate Community page
  • Updates to SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • Updates to SAP Activate for SAP Cloud ERP Private
  • Updates to SAP Activate for Conversion of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2-system to 3-system landscape
  • Updates to SAP Activate for SAP SuccessFactors
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Test Automation Tool 2608 Release

SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: AI-Assisted Tasks Now Available

SAP Activate now incorporates SAP Business AI across three dimensions: (1) guiding customers to adopt SAP Joule and Business AI capabilities during implementation, (2) supporting extensions built on the SAP Business AI Platform in line with clean core, and (3) leveraging AI to accelerate implementation activities across the project lifecycle. 

As part of this evolution, the first wave of AI-assisted implementation tasks was released earlier in June and enhanced during July, delivering 18 AI-assisted tasks, 21 new tested prompts, and updated accelerators. The initial focus is on the use of SAP Cloud ALM AI capabilities, SAP Joule for Consultants, and Test Automation Tool in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

They cover a range of project activities — from self-enablement and project artifact preparation to fit-to-standard workshop support, requirements generation, extension design, data migration planning, and testing. You can identify these tasks using the "AI-Assisted Procedure" tag in the SAP Activate road map viewer on SAP for Me, where SAP Joule is also available as a conversational assistant to help you navigate implementation guidance. 

Read the full blog post here for a detailed walkthrough of all three dimensions and how to get started.

SAP Activate Community | SAP Activate Blogs

Join the SAP Activate Community to explore valuable content and resources while connecting with SAP partners, clients, and internal colleagues by participating in discussions and sharing knowledge. Make sure to follow the SAP Activate tag to access blog posts like those highlighted here, along with additional insights shared by SAP Activate experts within the community.

Recap of some blog posts from the community:

Recap of some blog posts from the community:

  • SAP Business AI in SAP Activate: Three Dimensions of AI for Your Implementation by Jan Musil
  • Introducing the SAP Activate Prompt Library: Curated AI Prompts for your Implementation Project by Chandan Gurav
  • AI-Assisted Extensibility Planning & Design in SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Cloud by Kiran Bose
  • SAP Activate, AI and Data Management by Daniel Ciecko
  • Work Smarter with AI-Assisted Tasks in the SAP Activate Project Management Workstream by Christina Galbreath
  • Transform Your Fit-to-Standard Workshop Preparation with AI-Assisted Procedures by Jair Azevedo
  • New SAP Activate Roadmap for SAP Sales Cloud V2 by Chandan Gurav
  • SAP Activate for SAP SuccessFactors by user alejandroazca
  • Update on SAP Activate Road Map Viewer Accelerators for Central Finance by Carla Nascimento
  • Exploring the New Guided Walk-Thru for SAP Activate for SAP IBP by user MabelleRM
  • Quality Gates: Leveraging the continuous quality check (CQC) Service Report for Project Readiness by Christina Galbreath
  • Foundational News: Implementing SAP Business Data Cloud – New Roadmap Release in SAP Roadmap Viewer by Ariane Hoffman
  • Do you want to boost your SAP project skills with Agile & SAP Activate by Margit Wagner

Updates of SAP Activate for SAP S/HANA Cloud Public Edition in 2608

The SAP Activate for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud implementation roadmap has been enhanced based on product management input, system functionality changes, and customer feedback. Below are highlights for this release.

  • Expanded AI support: AI-assisted procedures have been added across multiple implementation tasks to help customers and consultants accelerate delivery using Business AI and Joule for Consultants. A new AI-Assisted Procedure tag and Prompt Library make AI-enabled guidance and prompts easier to find.
  • Business AI integration: AI-powered scenarios are now embedded throughout the implementation methodology, providing guidance at the point where it is most relevant.
  • Enhanced Fit-to-Standard guidance: New Fit-to-Standard overview video and RACI matrix help project teams better understand workshop activities and stakeholder responsibilities. See task Prepare for Fit-to-Standard Workshops.
  • Integrated Quality Gates: Quality Gates are now incorporated into the Continuous Quality Check (CQC) process, enabling customers to complete phase-end questionnaires directly in SAP Cloud ALM and submit them to SAP.

Improved upgrade methodology: The upgrade roadmap for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (3-system landscape) has been expanded with more detailed guidance and stronger project management support for major upgrade milestones. SAP Activate for the Upgrade of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (3-system landscape).

Updates of SAP Activate for Cloud ERP Private

SAP Activate, the standardized framework for the RISE with SAP Methodology, has been enhanced with numerous updates, as detailed in the release notes.

This release significantly expands AI capabilities by introducing AI-assisted procedures, prompt-based accelerators, and enhanced SAP Cloud ALM guidance that help consultants streamline project execution, improve documentation quality, and increase consistency using SAP Joule for Consultants. In addition, the release strengthens Clean Core integration guidance with the latest SAP recommendations and introduces new AI-powered learning and change management capabilities to accelerate end-user enablement and content development. 

Key highlights include:

  • Expanded SAP Business AI integration across the SAP Activate for SAP Cloud ERP Private methodology, embedding AI-assisted procedures and new prompt-based accelerators into key project lifecycle tasks — including backlog estimation, cutover planning, hypercare support, and operations handover — enabling consultants to accelerate execution and improve output quality with SAP Joule for Consultants.
  • Enhanced SAP Cloud ALM guidance with AI capabilities, adding new procedure steps and accelerators for document summarization and AI-assisted requirement generation across project setup, workshop execution, and requirements documentation tasks, helping project teams work faster and more consistently.
  • Strengthened Clean Core integration guidance by incorporating SAP Note 3690029 — Integration Technologies and Frameworks in Context of Clean Core Integration — across all PCE scenarios, ensuring consultants have up-to-date direction on compliant integration approaches aligned with clean core principles.

Improved learning and change management enablement by introducing AI-assisted accelerators for user learning and change management, including a new micro-app hub integration via SAP Discovery Center, supporting more targeted and efficient learning needs analysis and content development planning.

See the release notes for a complete list of all improvements. 

Updates of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2-system landscape to 3-system landscape 

We are happy to share the highlights of the latest improvements to the SAP Activate guidance for our customers converting their SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition environments from a 2-system landscape to a 2-system landscape.

These are the key updates:

  • Updated assignment of tasks to the “Day 1 Post-Conversion Checklist” tag to include the tasks added to the Realize phase to address specific post-conversion steps.
  • Multiple updates for test data refresh and test data conversion - revised procedure details in the tasks Prepare for Test Data Conversion Service and Prepare for Test Data Refresh Service. Additionally, refreshed the accelerator Test Data Conversion Checklist.
  • Added new task Copy Workforce Users and Request Test Data Refresh after the Conversion in the Realize phase, with additional details for the customer to copy workforce users and submit a request for TDR, including the addition of new accelerators.
  • Updated task Prepare for Test Data Refresh Service in the Prepare phase with information about how to request the service.
  • Updated task Prepare for Test Data Conversion Service in the Prepare phase with information about how to request the service.

Updates of SAP Activate for SAP SuccessFactors

The SAP Activate for SAP SuccessFactors roadmap has been enhanced based on product management input, system functionality changes, and customer feedback. Below are the highlights:

  • Integrated Quality Gates: Phase-end Quality Gates are now part of the Continuous Quality Check (CQC) process, allowing questionnaires to be completed and submitted directly through SAP Cloud ALM.
  • Business AI integration: AI-powered scenarios are now embedded throughout the implementation methodology to provide contextual guidance when teams need it.
  • Joule integration: New guidance has been added for configuring and enabling Joule as part of the implementation process. Joule integration.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Test Automation Tool 2608 Release

The CE2608 release introduces additional enhancements to simplify test scoping, improve test data management, and enable easier comparison test statuses across different Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) executions.

Key highlights include:

  • AI-Powered Test Scoping in Intelligent Test Scoper (i-TS)
    • Enhanced by combining AI with SAP Standard code-change analysis to automatically recommend the most relevant Post Upgrade Test (PUT) scope.
    • Smarter test scope recommendations: The AI model considers customer usage, process complexity, change-to-touch ratio, historical regression behavior, and SAP standard code changes to identify the optimal regression scope.
    • Reduced testing effort and costs: Customers can execute fewer, more relevant regression tests, reducing validation effort, shortening execution time, and lowering overall testing costs without compromising quality.
    • No additional customer action required: AI-based test scoping is enabled by default for Post Upgrade Tests, while still allowing customers to modify the proposed scope in the Manage Upgrade Test app if needed.
  • Simplifying Test Data Management with the Creation of Multiple Data Variants and Assigning them to TDC Variants
    • New Dataset entity for simplified test data management: A new Dataset feature groups multiple Test Data Container (TDC) variants into a reusable entity, making test data easier to organize and manage.
    • Create multiple data variants in one step: User can create and assign multiple data variants simultaneously, significantly reducing repetitive manual effort and speeding up test plan preparation.
    • Automatic assignment of test data: For test plans with multiple TDCs, selecting a dataset automatically maps the appropriate TDC variants, improving consistency and minimizing errors.
    • Greater efficiency and productivity: The enhancements streamline test data management, reduce manual work, improve organization, and allow testing teams to focus more on validating business processes.
  • Post Upgrade Tests (PUT) Comparator enhancements
    • Compare test executions across releases: The enhanced Post Upgrade Test (PUT) Comparator allows users to compare two completed executions of the same execution variant to quickly identify differences, analyze failures, and validate upgrade readiness.
    • Improved visibility and scalability: Enhanced comparison results and pagination make it easier to review and navigate large test execution datasets with hundreds of matching scope items.
    • Advanced filtering for faster analysis: New filtering capabilities enable users to quickly focus on failed, successful, or impacted scope items, reducing manual review effort.
    • Better usability and navigation: User interface improvements streamline comparison workflows, making Post Upgrade Test analysis faster and more intuitive.

Overall, these enhancements significantly improve testing efficiency, increase data accuracy, reduce manual effort, and boost productivity across post-upgrade test automation workflows. For more details, refer to the blog.

 

Please share your feedback and questions in the comments to this blog post or directly via the feedback function in the SAP Activate road map viewer on SAP for Me.

Run simple – SAP Activate – Accelerate and simplify the adoption of SAP S/4HANA Cloud.

entry.title: F110 Automatic Vendor Bank Selection for ACH/Wire Payments Across Multiple Company Codes
entry.content: Vendor has multiple bank accounts (ACH and Wire) maintained in vendor master data. The same vendor is used across multiple company codes. Invoice posting is being automated through OCR/AI, so manual selection of Partner Bank Type (BVTYP) is no longer reliable. We need SAP ECC F110 to automatically determine the correct vendor bank account based on company code and/or payment method. Is there a standard configuration available before considering a custom enhancement?

entry.title: Error AADSTS90072: when a newly provisioned SAP Learning Practice System
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

I would appreciate any advice regarding an issue with a newly provisioned SAP Learning Practice System.

My SAP Learning account is working correctly, and I can log in without any issues using my corporate email 

Last week, I was able to access and use my previous Practice System normally. After that system expired, I provisioned a new Practice System. Since then, whenever I click "Access", I receive the following error:

AADSTS90072: User account 'xxxxxxx@xxx.com' does not exist in tenant 'SAP SE' and cannot access the application.

 

I have already:
- Tried an incognito/private browser session
- Signed out and signed in again
- Verified that SAP Learning access is working normally

The issue only occurs when launching the newly provisioned Practice System.

I have already created a case through SAP Learning Self-Service, but I am frustrated by the delay in receiving support, as I am currently unable to continue my hands-on learning activities while waiting for a response.

Has anyone experienced the same issue after provisioning a new Practice System? If so, was there any workaround or did SAP need to reprovision the environment?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

Best regards,
Wellington Delmondes

entry.title: Re: Subsequent carrier charge (delta only) after first fr...
entry.content:
Hi! 

I'll try help You. Please check snote 3065464 with defines the Basic vs Advanced. But not at all its explicit explainned on. 

  1.  For a delta-only subsequent charge, is there any Basic TM way to add it to the existing freight order?
    -This is not explicitly stated in Note 3065464. Therefore, I believe we can consider it part of the basic scope.
  2. Does adding a new charge line to an already settled freight order require the Advanced insert API?
    -No. Basic TM without API .  It's a common changes on document
  3. Is a Service Order against the original freight order a valid Basic TM alternative for accessorial charges?
    - Advanced :  It is explicitly stated in the note.  "TOR Category: Service order from Header or Item can be created out of a TOR document " - 

    BR,
     Jefferson Alcântara

entry.title: Re: SAP TM solution for Voyage Chartering, Costing and Pr...
entry.content:
Hi! 

Your main question its about Cost Distribution.
With TM you can transport commodities, settle cost freight and post the cost distribution to material valuation or for expensing at the product level.
On costr distribution you'll can find material, SO, customer, order Booking or Freight order, cost center, etc..
I think this link can help you clarify your doubts:
https://learning.sap.com/courses/charges-and-settlement-in-sap-s-4hana-transportation-management/distributing-freight-costs-1

But "commodity costs" isnt a core of TM!  TM post only transportation charges! 
BR,

entry.title: Re: Unable to Set Payment Terms and Payment Method for Ve...
entry.content:
Dear @dawid90,
Thank you for the detailed explanation.

I was able to identify the reason why the payment terms were not inherited. Instead of entering a Residual Amount, I had entered the difference using the Post on Account tab, which resulted in a new supplier open item being created. That was the cause of the issue.

I have one additional question.

When I entered a Residual Amount and posted the transaction, the resulting supplier residual item was created as a negative debit amount. Is this standard system behavior, or is there any way to have the residual item posted instead as a positive credit amount?

Thank you in advance for your clarification.

entry.title: BW Infocube Compression request taking more disk space
entry.content:
Recently, we completed the BW compression activity for the years DIGITS to DIGITS in the Q system to reduce memory disk space.
However, we have noticed that the disk space has unexpectedly increased compared to the disk usage before the compression activity.
Could you please advise if there are any temporary files, log files, or other system files that can be safely cleaned up to reduce the disk space usage?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: BW Infocube Compression request taking more disk space
entry.content:
From HANA DB poing of view, I think you could regularly archive data as mentioned in this note:

2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables
-> /<bw_prefix>/F*

=>
3232938 - [BW Central KBA] How-To: Data Management for BW Infoprovider tables

This is a DB relevant note, however the involved tool/report that is more close to application, you may need contact your BW admin for data archiving.

The meanwhile, I think it is better for you to schedule a redistribution for the whole system in case this has not been performed.

- note
1908075 - SAP BW on SAP HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution
2334091 - SAP BW/4HANA: Table Placement and Landscape Redistribution

The Redistribution will handle all tables in this system, and this is stongly suggested for BW system.

entry.title: Re: Concern with program asset Year-End Closing program i...
entry.content:
Hi!

It is always better to executed Transaction Code AJAB to close Fiscal Year in Asset Accounting.

By executing AJAB, program FAA_CLOSE_FISCAL_YEARS will be called as of S/4HANA 2020 onwards.

Previously was indeed RAJABS00.

entry.title: Clarification Required – Joule Premium for Customer Experience Activation Message
entry.content:
Hi SAP Support Team,

We are seeing the following message in SAP for Me:

Joule Premium for Customer Experience – Activation Required

The page shows:

  • AI Units Consumed: 8
  • Entitled Requests: 5,200 per month
  • Excess-use charging may apply if the request limit is exceeded

We are primarily using SAP SuccessFactors and have not intentionally requested activation of the Joule Premium for Customer Experience package.

Please clarify:

  1. Why is this activation message appearing for our account?
  2. Is this package already included in our current contract or entitlement?
  3. Will activating the package result in any additional charges?
  4. How are the 8 AI Units calculated and deducted?
  5. What excess-use charges would apply if the 5,200 monthly requests are exceeded?
  6. Does simply activating the package create any financial commitment?
  7. Is this package relevant to SuccessFactors, or should we use Joule Premium for Human Capital Management instead?

Please review our account entitlement and confirm the commercial impact before we proceed with activation.

 

Thanks,
Aji


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

Hi SAP Support Team,

We are seeing the following message in SAP for Me:

Joule Premium for Customer Experience – Activation Required

We are primarily using SAP SuccessFactors and have not intentionally requested activation of the Joule Premium for Customer Experience package.

Please clarify:

  1. Why is this activation message appearing for our account?
  2. Is this package already included in our current contract or entitlement?
  3. Will activating the package result in any additional charges?
  4. How are the 8 AI Units calculated and deducted?
  5. What excess-use charges would apply if the 5,200 monthly requests are exceeded?
  6. Does simply activating the package create any financial commitment?
  7. Is this package relevant to SuccessFactors, or should we use Joule Premium for Human Capital Management instead?

Please review our account entitlement and confirm the commercial impact before we proceed with activation.

 

 

 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Price element on service quotation header
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We are implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for the Service line of business. Our customer has a business scenario that requires adding price conditions at the header level of a service quotation in order to apply a manually entered percentage discount.

However, when we try to add a price element at the header level, the system displays the following warning message:

You cannot add Price Element at header level.

Please see the screenshot below.

Is it not possible to add price conditions at the header level of service quotations and service orders in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, or is this a configurable restriction?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best regards,

David Avila.
